event: Early hominins inhabit the Afar region near present-day Djibouti, part of the East African cradle of humankind that yielded some of the world's oldest human ancestor fossils.
Anatomically modern humans cross the region around the southern Red Sea, with the Bab-el-Mandeb strait serving as a likely corridor for the early migration of humans out of Africa.
Lake Abhe and the Gobaad plain support Neolithic communities, leaving rock art and stone tools as Stone Age hunter-gatherers exploit the area's lakes and grasslands.
Pastoralists herding cattle and small livestock spread across the Horn of Africa, leaving prehistoric petroglyphs depicting animals at sites such as Balho and Dorra.
The region forms part of the fabled Land of Punt, a trading partner of ancient Egypt prized for frankincense, myrrh, gold, and exotic goods.
Queen Hatshepsut of Egypt dispatches a famous maritime expedition to the Land of Punt, recorded on the walls of her temple at Deir el-Bahari.
Trade across the Gulf of Aden and Red Sea links the Horn of Africa with South Arabia, fostering early Afro-Semitic cultural and commercial exchange.
Coastal settlements participate in Indian Ocean and Red Sea commerce later described in the Greek mariner's guide the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea.
The port of Zeila and nearby Red Sea harbors grow as nodes in the trade network of the Aksumite Empire and Indian Ocean merchants.
Islam spreads peacefully into the region through Arab traders and missionaries, with early mosques established among the coastal and Somali populations.
The Ifat and later Adal sultanates develop in the broader region, drawing the area into Muslim Horn of Africa polities centered on trade through Zeila.
The Sultanate of Adal consolidates power across the northern Horn, controlling trade and contesting the Christian Ethiopian highlands.
Imam Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi (Ahmad Gran) leads the Adal Sultanate in a major war against the Ethiopian Empire, mobilizing forces from across the region.
The Adal Sultanate fragments and the Sultanate of Tadjoura emerges as an Afar coastal polity controlling caravan trade to the interior.
France purchases the anchorage of Obock from local Afar sultans, establishing its first foothold on the Gulf of Aden coast.
France begins extending protectorate treaties over Afar and Issa Somali chiefs, expanding its influence along the coast during the Scramble for Africa.
France founds the city of Djibouti, which becomes the administrative and commercial center of the colony for its superior natural harbor.
France formally organizes its possessions as French Somaliland (Cote francaise des Somalis), with Leonce Lagarde as governor.
Djibouti city is made the capital of French Somaliland, replacing Obock as the colony's seat of government.
Construction begins on the Franco-Ethiopian Railway linking the port of Djibouti to the Ethiopian interior, a key economic lifeline.
The Franco-Ethiopian Railway reaches Addis Ababa, cementing Djibouti's role as Ethiopia's principal outlet to the sea.
Djibouti gains strategic importance as Italy invades Ethiopia, with the colony serving as a transit and supply route during the conflict.
After France's fall in World War II, French Somaliland aligns with the Vichy regime and is blockaded by British and Free French forces.
French Somaliland rallies to the Free French and Allied side following a prolonged blockade and the defection of colonial troops.
In a referendum, French Somaliland votes to remain a territory within the French Community rather than join newly independent Somalia.
A second referendum favors continued association with France, and the territory is renamed the French Territory of the Afars and the Issas amid ethnic tensions and protests.
France signals willingness to grant independence as nationalist agitation grows, led by figures such as Hassan Gouled Aptidon.
Djibouti gains full independence from France on 27 June, with Hassan Gouled Aptidon becoming the country's first president.
Djibouti is admitted to the United Nations, the Organisation of African Unity, and the Arab League shortly after independence.
President Gouled establishes the Rassemblement Populaire pour le Progres (RPP) as the sole legal party, entrenching one-party rule.
Djibouti is formally declared a one-party state under the RPP following the banning of opposition parties.
Djibouti hosts the founding of the Intergovernmental Authority on Drought and Development (IGADD), later IGAD, headquartered in the capital.
Civil war erupts as the Afar-led Front for the Restoration of Unity and Democracy (FRUD) launches an insurgency against the government.
A new constitution is adopted by referendum, introducing limited multiparty politics with up to four legal parties.
A peace agreement with a main faction of FRUD ends the most intense phase of the civil war and brings rebels into the government.
Ismail Omar Guelleh succeeds his uncle Hassan Gouled Aptidon as president, winning the country's first contested presidential election.
A final peace accord with the remaining FRUD faction definitively ends the civil war and integrates former rebels into national institutions.
Camp Lemonnier becomes a major United States military base, making Djibouti a key hub for American counterterrorism operations in the region.
Djibouti holds its first multiparty parliamentary elections under a coalition system as the country opens its economy and ports to foreign investment.
Ismail Omar Guelleh is re-elected president in a vote boycotted by the opposition, extending his hold on power.
A border clash with Eritrea at Ras Doumeira erupts into armed conflict, prompting international mediation over the disputed frontier.
A constitutional amendment removes presidential term limits, allowing Guelleh to seek further terms in office.
Guelleh wins a third term amid Arab Spring-inspired protests, while a severe Horn of Africa drought strains the country.
Djibouti signs an agreement allowing China to establish a military base, its first overseas, reflecting the nation's growing geostrategic importance.
China opens its overseas military base in Djibouti, joining French, American, Japanese, and other foreign forces stationed in the country.
The Chinese-built Addis Ababa-Djibouti standard-gauge electric railway begins operations, modernizing the vital trade link to Ethiopia.
Djibouti opens the multi-billion-dollar Doraleh Multipurpose Port and free-trade zone, expanding its ambition to be a regional logistics hub.
Ismail Omar Guelleh wins a fifth term as president, continuing more than two decades in power.
